channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Oil Begins Flowing Through California Pipeline Under Trump Order
item.description: Oil had not flowed through the pipeline since a 2015 rupture caused an environmental disaster on California’s Central Coast. It sets up a new fight between the Trump administration and state officials.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:description: A leaky pipeline was the source of a spill, seen in this 2015 file photo, that polluted Refugio State Beach, north of Goleta, Calif., and killed hundreds of birds and marine mammals. The pipeline’s new owner, Sable Offshore, says the problems have been fixed and that it has restarted the flow of oil at the direction of the Trump administration.

item.title: University of Florida Penalizes GOP Group Over Antisemitism Accusations
item.description: After the University of Florida restricted the Republican organization from operating on campus, the group sued the university arguing its First Amendment rights were violated.
item.dc:creator: Dana Goldstein
item.media:credit: Jacob Langston for The New York Times
item.media:description: The University of Florida’s interim president, Donald W. Landry, said the school supports its Jewish students.

item.title: Kouri Richins Found Guilty of Poisoning Her Husband in Utah Murder Trial
item.description: Kouri Richins was accused of mixing a lethal dose of fentanyl into a drink she made for her husband. She later wrote a children’s book about coping with grief.
item.dc:creator: Remy Tumin
item.media:description: Kouri Richins, a Utah mother accused of fatally poisoning her husband, during her murder trial at the Summit County Courthouse in Park City, Utah.

item.title: Richard Grenell: From Trump Cabinet Hopeful to Ex-Kennedy Center Manager
item.description: Richard Grenell once hoped to be President Trump’s secretary of state. Instead, Mr. Trump just replaced him as Kennedy Center president.
item.dc:creator: Elizabeth Williamson
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: Richard Grenell, left, the outgoing president of the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts, was seated next to his replacement, Matt Floca, the center’s vice president of facilities operations, at a White House news conference on Monday.

item.title: Trump Claims an Ex-President Confided His Regrets on Iran. But Who?
item.description: The New York Times reached out to people close to President Trump’s predecessors. They disputed Mr. Trump’s claims.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House on Monday.

item.title: Aide Accused by Hegseth of Leaking Is Hired for Intelligence Job
item.description: Dan Caldwell will serve as an adviser to senior intelligence officials. His hiring signals the end of a high-profile investigation.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe and Julian E. Barnes
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:credit: Justin T. Gellerson for The New York Times
item.media:description: An investigation turned up no proof that Dan Caldwell or two other Trump administration officials committed any wrongdoing.

item.title: Gregory Bovino to Retire From Border Patrol
item.description: The outspoken border official drove the agency’s aggressive immigration policy in U.S. cities, including Minneapolis, until January.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:description: Gregory Bovino had a decades-long career with the U.S. Border Patrol. His brash style drew criticism from both the left and the right.

item.title: Inside the Democrats’ Infighting in the Illinois Primary
item.description: We talked to our Peoria-born reporter about Primary Day in what he claims is America’s best state.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:description: From left, Representative Robin Kelly, Representative Raja Krishnamoorthi and Lt. Gov. Juliana Stratton are running in the Democratic primary for Senate in Illinois.

item.title: War Isn’t the Only Thing on Trump’s Mind
item.description: President Trump spoke to journalists for about 40 minutes before holding a news conference on Monday, offering a glimpse into his priorities.
item.dc:creator: Erica L. Green
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:description: President Trump on Monday at the White House, where he went on a series of tangents that were notable even for a president who boasts about his oratorical “weave.”

item.title: Fire on the U.S. Aircraft Carrier Gerald R. Ford Raged for Hours, Sailors Say
item.description: The Ford is now entering its 10th month of deployment after arriving in the Middle East from the Caribbean.
item.dc:creator: Helene Cooper
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The crew on the U.S.S. Gerald R. Ford have been told that their deployment will likely be extended into May, which would put them at an entire year at sea.

item.title: S.E.C.’s Enforcement Chief Abruptly Resigns
item.description: Margaret A. Ryan is leaving the agency just six months after joining it, a surprise given that most people hold the position for years.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess
item.media:credit: Alyssa Schukar for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Securities and Exchange Commission headquarters in Washington last year.

item.title: Supreme Court Defers Decision on Trump’s Bid to End Protections for Migrants
item.description: The justices will hear arguments over the president’s efforts to terminate the program, known as Temporary Protected Status, for migrants from Haiti and Syria as part of his mass deportation efforts.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: A vigil for Haitians living in the United States under Temporary Protected Status in Miami last month.

item.title: Colorado Funeral Home Owner Is Sentenced to 18 Years on Federal Fraud Charges
item.description: Carie Hallford, 49, and her husband failed to provide cremation and burial services they had promised to grieving families, prosecutors said.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.category: Return to Nature Funeral Home (HallfordHomes LLC)
item.media:credit: Jerilee Bennett/The Gazette, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The Return to Nature Funeral Home in Penrose, Colo., about 100 miles south of Denver.

item.title: Trump Officials Look to More Managed Approach to Trade With China
item.description: The administration has begun discussing a new “Board of Trade” with China to try to balance what U.S. officials see as an unequal economic relationship.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson
item.media:description: Scott Bessent, the Treasury secretary, left, and Jamieson Greer, the U.S. trade representative, conducted talks with their Chinese counterparts on Sunday and Monday in Paris.

item.title: What Are Trump’s Positions on Iran? They Can Change by the Sentence.
item.description: The president is no stranger to staking out contradictory stands, part of what his aides say is his negotiating style. But on Iran, his shifting positions are colliding with the consequences of war.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump leaving the White House last month. The conflict in Iran has come with consequences including a rising death toll and soaring gas prices.

item.title: In Choosing ‘Epic Fury,’ Trump Names a War and Defines His Presidency
item.description: The branding of the U.S. military operation against Iran is a quintessentially Trumpian choice for a leader whose tenure has been marked by anger.
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Trump Airs Rep. Neal Dunn’s Terminal Diagnosis, Claiming to Have Reversed It
item.description: The president leaned on House Speaker Mike Johnson to name a Florida Republican whose grave prognosis was imperiling the party’s already slim voting margin. Both said his prospects had improved.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:description: Representative Neal Dunn of Florida at an event in Dayton, Ohio, last year. At the White House on Monday, President Trump aired the congressman’s personal health diagnosis.

item.title: David A. Keene, Leader of Two Right-Wing Groups, Dies at 80
item.description: He led the N.R.A. and, for 29 years, the American Conservative Union, which organizes the influential annual Conservative Political Action Conference, or CPAC. He was also a columnist.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:credit: Christian Gooden/St. Louis Post-Dispatch, via Associated Press
item.media:description: David A. Keene at the National Rifle Association’s national convention in 2012, during his term as the group’s president.

item.title: Appeals Court Allows Trump Administration’s Third-Country Deportations, for Now
item.description: The administration’s policy of deporting people to South Sudan, Rwanda and other distant countries has been a striking attempt to create uncertainty for immigrants.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:description: Migrants landing in Costa Rica last year after being deported from the United States.

item.title: Susie Wiles, Trump’s White House Chief of Staff, Has Breast Cancer
item.description: Ms. Wiles said that the disease was caught in its early stages and that she would remain in her role.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I am grateful to have an outstanding team of doctors who detected the cancer early and are guiding my care, and I am encouraged by a strong prognosis,” Susie Wiles, the White House chief of staff, said in a statement.

item.title: In Deep-Red Idaho, a Republican Rift Over Schools and ‘Parental Choice’
item.description: Does “choice” in Idaho mean vouchers for private-school tuition or publicly funded remote learning that has brought AP classes and advanced math to the state’s rural reaches?
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: The town of Carey in rural Blaine County is nestled in a valley in Idaho.

item.title: Why Republicans are Changing Course on Immigration
item.description: What did Speaker Mike Johnson mean when he talked about a “course correction” in the Trump administration’s deportation approach? Our White House correspondent Zolan Kanno-Youngs analyzes how the message around deportation is changing.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, Gilad Thaler, Nikolay Nikolov, June Kim and Luke Piotrowski

item.title: With Iran War, Trump Risks Stepping on Gains From His Own Tax Cuts
item.description: President Trump’s war in Iran has raised some costs just as many Americans are starting to see savings from last year’s tax cuts.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The dominant impression of the economy for many Americans this spring could come down to rapidly rising gas prices.

item.title: Trump’s Effort to Target Rivals Stall as Judges Cut Short Basic Investigative Steps
item.description: A ruling Friday that derailed an investigation into the Federal Reserve chair at an exceptionally early stage showed the limits of President Trump’s campaign of legal retribution.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Schmidt and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: Judge James E. Boasberg ruled on Friday that there was “essentially zero evidence to suspect” that Jerome H. Powell, the Federal Reserve chair, had committed a crime.

item.title: In Illinois, Krishnamoorthi’s Indian American Heritage Shapes Bid
item.description: On Tuesday, Representative Raja Krishnamoorthi is looking to take a major step toward becoming only the second Indian American elected to the Senate.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rep. Raja Krishnamoorthi marches with other Illinois politicians in the annual Chicago St. Patrick’s Day Parade on Saturday.

item.title: In Tense Meeting, Mehmet Oz Pressed Medical Societies on Trans Care for Teens
item.description: Most groups defended their support for medical intervention. But the Society for Plastic Surgeons broke with the consensus.
item.dc:creator: Jeremy W. Peters
item.category: Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services
item.media:description: Mehmet Oz, the head of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services, called in the leaders of American Medical Association, the American Society of Plastic Surgeons and the American Psychiatric Association, among others.

item.title: Republican Officials Fuel Vitriol Against Muslims and Islamic School in Alabama
item.description: A local campaign against the small school reflects growing Islamophobia in conservative enclaves in America and among G.O.P. officials.
item.dc:creator: Robert Draper
item.media:credit: Wes Frazer for The New York Times
item.media:description: Students at the Islamic Academy of Alabama walking to classes held inside mobile buildings due to the school not having enough space for all its needs.

item.title: Quartz Countertop Makers Want Immunity Against Legal Claims from Sick Stone Cutters
item.description: Workers are filing lawsuits against the countertop industry as cases of silicosis, a deadly lung disease, rise.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien
item.media:credit: Luke Sharrett for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jeff Rose, a part owner of a countertop fabrication shop in Nicholasville, Ky., is among the hundreds, if not thousands, of workers who have been diagnosed with silicosis, a lethal lung disease caused by inhaling silica dust.

item.title: Professors Are Changing What They Teach, Even Far From Trump’s Gaze
item.description: Harvard is the White House’s biggest target, but professors all over the country have been censoring themselves, avoiding provocative topics and rewriting grants.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Tony Luong for The New York Times
item.media:description: Schools in more liberal places, including Northwestern in Illinois and Brown in Rhode Island, have sometimes acceded to some of the federal government’s demands.

item.title: The N.B.A.’s Curry Family Royalty, Market Moves and the Inside Scoop on N.F.L. Free Agency
item.description: Steph and Dell Curry sit down with The Athletic’s Marcus Thompson.
item.dc:creator: Robert Mays, Zena Keita, Jason Goff, Maggie Burbank, Jessie Burton, Craig Custance, Stephen Lorenzo and Andrew Liu
